en.extract:
The Antarctic petrel (Thalassoica antarctica) is a boldly marked dark brown and white petrel, found in Antarctica, most commonly in the Ross and Weddell Seas. They eat Antarctic krill, fish, and small squid. They feed while swimming but can dive from both the surface and the air.


Taxonomy and systematics
Captain James Cook saw the Antarctic petrel on his second voyage to the south Pacific. In 1773 both Cook and the naturalist Georg Forster mentioned the petrel in their separate accounts of the voyage. Forster wrote:

On the 17th, in the forenoon, we crossed the antarctic circle, and advanced into the southern frigid zone, which had hitherto remained impenetrable to all navigators. Some days before this period we had seen a new species of petrel, of a brown colour, with a white belly and rump, and a large white spot on the wings, which we now named the antarctic petrel, as we saw great flights of twenty on thirty of them hereabouts, of which we shot many that
unfortunately never fell into the ship.
Based on these reports in 1783 the French polymath Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon included the petrel as "Le pétrel antarctique ou Damier brun" in his Histoire Naturelle des Oiseaux. The species was also included by John Latham in his A General Synopsis of Birds. When the German naturalist Johann Friedrich Gmelin updated Carl Linnaeus's Systema Naturae in 1789 he included a brief description of the Antarctic petrel, coined the binomial name Procellaria antarctica and cited the earlier authors. The Antarctic petrel is now the only species placed in the genus Thalassoica that was introduced in 1853 by the German naturalist Ludwig Reichenbach. The genus name combines the Ancient Greek thalassa meaning "sea" with oikos meaning "house". The species is monotypic: no subspecies are recognised. The word petrel is derived from St. Peter and the story of his walking on water. This is in reference to the petrel's habit of appearing to run on the water to take off.
The Antarctic petrel is placed in the family Procellariidae of the order Procellariiformes. This petrel along with the snow petrel, the Cape petrel, the giant petrels, and the fulmars, are considered to be a different subclade from the other Procellariidae members. They share certain identifying features. First, they have nasal passages that attach to the upper bill called naricorns. Although the nostrils on the petrels are on the top of the upper bill. The bills of Procellariiformes are also unique in that they are split into between seven and nine horny plates. On petrels, one of these plates forms the hooked portion of the upper bill. They produce a stomach oil made up of wax esters and triglycerides that is stored in the proventriculus. This can be sprayed out of their mouths as a defence against predators and as an energy rich food source for chicks and for the adults during their long flights. Finally, they also have a salt gland that is situated above the nasal passage and helps desalinate their bodies, due to the high amount of ocean water that they imbibe. It excretes a high saline solution from their nose.


Description

The adult Antarctic petrel has a brown head, sides, throat, and back. The bill is dark brown and the feet are grey. The underparts are white and their tail and secondaries on the wings are white with brown tips. These are medium-sized relative to other petrels with a wingspan of 100–110 cm (39–43 in), a length of 40–45 cm (16–18 in), and an average weight of 675 g (23.8 oz).


Distribution and habitat
The Antarctic petrel, as its name implies, lives and breeds in the Southern Ocean, and on the Antarctic islands. They nest on snow-free cliffs and rock faces, on the coast or on offshore islands. However, they have been found up to 250 km inland. Another common roosting spot is icebergs. Breeding colonies during the October–November breeding period, can be as large as 200,000 pairs. However, the largest single colony ever observed, Mühlig-Hofmann Mountains, was estimated to be about one million Antartic petrels, presumably including non-breeders and chicks. 
They occasionally migrate to Australia or New Zealand in late winter. Unfortunately, this tends to occur when they're caught in a bad storm.


Behaviour


Food and feeding
The petrel's diet is mainly krill, squid and small fish. Food is usually seized when the bird is on the surface but they also plunge-dive to obtain food, diving up to a depth of 1.5 m (4 ft 11 in).


Breeding
The Antarctic petrel breeding period is during October–November. Each pair lays a single egg, which they incubate for 45–48 days after which there is a 42-47 day nestling period. Both members of the pair incubate the egg, 4% of pairs are female-female.
Eggs have a 70-90% hatching rate. The two main causes of egg loss were predation by South polar skuas, and an egg rolling out of the nest and freezing.
Antarctic petrel chicks rely on their parents for food as well as warmth. The physiological condition of the parent petrel dictates the amount of food it provides to its chick.  Provisioning by parent petrels depends on both their own body condition and their chick's needs. Parent petrels in better body condition were more likely to have a chick that survived, and were able to increase the amount of food they gave to a smaller chick in a cross-fostering experiment. Chicks become thermally independent after day 11 post-hatching.


Status
This petrel has an estimated occurrence range of 77,500,000 km2 (29,922,917 sq mi) and between 10 and 20 million adult birds. Due to its huge range and large numbers, it has been classified by the International Union for Conservation of Nature as a species of least concern.


References


External links
Antarctic petrel photos

Its head, body, and the top half of its wings are dark brown. Its tail and the bottom half of its wings are white. | parent_authority = [[Ludwig Reichenbach|Reichenbach]], 1853 | taxon = Thalassoica antarctica | authority = ([[Johann Friedrich Gmelin|Gmelin, JF]], 1789) | range_map = Thalassoica antarctica map.svg | range_map_caption = {{leftlegend|#8888df|range|outline=gray}} }} The '''Antarctic petrel''' ('''''Thalassoica antarctica''''') is a boldly marked dark brown and white [[petrel]], found in Antarctica, most commonly in the [[Ross Sea|Ross]] and [[Weddell Sea]]s. They eat Antarctic [[krill]], [[fish]], and small [[squid]]. They feed while swimming but can dive from both the surface and the air. ==Taxonomy and systematics== [[James Cook|Captain James Cook]] saw the Antarctic petrel on his [[Second voyage of James Cook|second voyage]] to the south Pacific. In 1773 both Cook and the naturalist [[Georg Forster]] mentioned the petrel in their separate accounts of the voyage.{{cite book|last1=Cook|first1=James|url=https://archive.org/details/b30413953_0001/page/257/mode/1up|title=A voyage towards the South Pole, and round the world: performed in His Majesty's ships the Resolution and Adventure, in the years 1772, 1773, 1774, and 1775|last2=Furneaux|first2=Tobias|date=1777|publisher=W. James Cook, During the Years 1772, 3, 4, and 5|date=1777|publisher=B. White, P. Elmsly, G. Robinson|volume=1|location=London|page=108|author-link=Georg Forster}} Forster wrote:
On the 17th, in the forenoon, we crossed the antarctic circle, and advanced into the southern frigid zone, which had hitherto remained impenetrable to all navigators. Some days before this period we had seen a new species of petrel, of a brown colour, with a white belly and rump, and a large white spot on the wings, which we now named the antarctic petrel, as we saw great flights of twenty on thirty of them hereabouts, of which we shot many that unfortunately never fell into the ship.
Based on these reports in 1783 the French polymath [[Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon]] included the petrel as "Le pétrel antarctique ou Damier brun" in his ''Histoire Naturelle des Oiseaux''.{{cite book|last=Buffon|first=Georges-Louis Leclerc de|title=Histoire Naturelle des Oiseaux|publisher=De l'Imprimerie Royale|year=1783|volume=9|location=Paris|pages=311–313|language=French|chapter=Le Pétrel Antarctique ou Damier brun|author-link=Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon|chapter-url=https://gallica.bnf.fr/ark:/12148/bpt6k1069724f/f367.item}} The species was also included by [[John Latham (ornithologist)|John Latham]] in his ''A General Synopsis of Birds''.{{cite book|last=Latham|first=John|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40079217|title=A General Synopsis of Birds|publisher=Printed for Leigh and Sotheby|year=1785|volume=3 part 2|location=London|page=400|author-link=John Latham (ornithologist)}} When the German naturalist [[Johann Friedrich Gmelin]] updated [[Carl Linnaeus]]'s ''[[Systema Naturae]]'' in 1789 he included a brief description of the Antarctic petrel, coined the [[binomial name]] ''Procellaria antarctica'' and cited the earlier authors.{{cite book|last=Gmelin|first=Johann Friedrich|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/25749943|title=Systema naturae per regna tria naturae: secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis|publisher=Georg. Emanuel. Beer|year=1789|edition=13th|volume=1 Part 2|location=Lipsiae [Leipzig]|page=565|language=Latin|author-link=Johann Friedrich Gmelin}} The Antarctic petrel is now the only species placed in the genus ''Thalassoica'' that was introduced in 1853 by the German naturalist [[Ludwig Reichenbach]].{{ cite book | last=Reichenbach | first=Ludwig | author-link=Ludwig Reichenbach | year=1853 | title=Handbuch der speciellen Ornithologie | volume=1 | language=German | location=Dresden und Leipzig | publisher=Expedition Vollständigsten Naturgeschichte | page=vi | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47618634 }} For the publication date see: {{ cite book | last1=Dickinson | first1=E.C. | author1-link=Edward C. This is in reference to the petrel's habit of appearing to run on the water to take off.{{cite book|last1=Gotch|first1=A. F.|title=Latin Names Explained A Guide to the Scientific Classifications of Reptiles, Birds & Mammals|publisher=Facts on File|year=1995|isbn=0-8160-3377-3|location=New York, NY|pages=191–192|chapter=Albatrosses, Fulmars, Shearwaters, and Petrels|orig-date=1979}} The Antarctic petrel is placed in the [[family (biology)|family]] [[Procellariidae]] of the [[order (biology)|order]] [[Procellariiformes]]. This petrel along with the [[snow petrel]], the [[Cape petrel]], the [[giant petrel]]s, and the [[fulmars]], are considered to be a different subclade from the other [[Procellariidae]] members.{{cite web|author=Tree of Life|date=27 Jun 2008|title=Procellariidae. Shearwaters, Petrels|url=http://tolweb.org/Procellariidae/26384|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090215055429/http://tolweb.org/Procellariidae/26384|archive-date=15 February 2009|access-date=18 Mar 2009|work=Tree of Life Web Project}} They share certain identifying features. First, they have nasal passages that attach to the upper [[beak|bill]] called [[naricorns]]. Although the nostrils on the petrels are on the top of the upper bill. The bills of Procellariiformes are also unique in that they are split into between seven and nine horny plates. On petrels, one of these plates forms the hooked portion of the upper bill. They produce a [[stomach oil]] made up of [[wax ester]]s and [[triglycerides]] that is stored in the [[proventriculus]]. This can be sprayed out of their mouths as a defence against predators and as an energy rich food source for chicks and for the adults during their long flights.{{cite encyclopedia|year=2003|title=Procellariiformes (Tubenosed Seabirds)|encyclopedia=Grzimek's Animal Life Encyclopedia|publisher=Gale Group|location=Farmington Hills, MI|last=Double|first=M. C.|others=Joseph E. Trumpey, Chief Scientific Illustrator|editor1-last=Hutchins|editor1-first=Michael|edition=2nd|volume=8, Birds I: Tinamous and Ratites to Hoatzins|pages=107–111|isbn=0-7876-5784-0|editor4-first=Donna|editor4-last=Olendorf|editor3-last=Bock|editor3-first=Walter J.|editor2-last=Jackson|editor2-first=Jerome A.}} Finally, they also have a [[salt gland]] that is situated above the nasal passage and helps desalinate their bodies, due to the high amount of ocean water that they imbibe. It excretes a high saline solution from their nose.{{cite book|last1=Ehrlich|first1=Paul R.|url=https://archive.org/details/birdershandbookf00ehrl_0|title=The Birders Handbook|last2=Dobkin|first2=David, S.|last3=Wheye|first3=Darryl|publisher=Simon & Schuster|year=1988|isbn=0-671-65989-8|edition=First|location=New York, NY|pages=[https://archive.org/details/birdershandbookf00ehrl_0/page/29 29]–31|url-access=registration}} ==Description== {{Multiple image | image1 = Antarctic Petrel 2.jpg | caption1 = Antarctic petrel off of the Western Antarctic Peninsula | image2 = Antarctic Petrel 3.jpg | direction = vertical }} The adult Antarctic petrel has a brown head, sides, throat, and back. The [[beak|bill]] is dark brown and the feet are grey. The underparts are white and their tail and [[remiges|secondaries]] on the wings are white with brown tips. These are medium-sized relative to other petrels with a wingspan of {{cvt|100|-|110|cm}}, a length of {{cvt|40|-|45|cm}}, and an average weight of {{cvt|675|g|}}.{{cite book | year=1990 | chapter=''Thalassoica antarctica'' Antarctic Petrel | editor1-last=Marchant | editor1-first=S. | editor2-last=Higgins | editor2-first=P.G. | title=Handbook of Australian, New Zealand & Antarctic Birds. Volume 1: Ratites to ducks; Part A, Ratites to petrels | location=Melbourne, Victoria | publisher=Oxford University Press | isbn=978-0-19-553068-1 | pages=384–391 | chapter-url=https://nzbirdsonline.org.nz/sites/all/files/066_Antarctic%20Petrel.pdf }} ==Distribution and habitat== The Antarctic petrel, as its name implies, lives and breeds in the [[Southern Ocean]], and on the Antarctic islands.{{cite book|last1=Clements|first1=James|title=The Clements Checklist of the Birds of the World|publisher=Cornell University Press|year=2007|isbn=978-0-8014-4501-9|edition=6th|location=Ithaca, NY|author-link=James Clements (ornithologist)}} They nest on snow-free cliffs and rock faces, on the coast or on offshore islands. However, they have been found up to 250 km inland. Another common roosting spot is icebergs.{{Cite web|title=Antarctic petrel|url=https://www.antarctica.gov.au/about-antarctica/animals/flying-birds/petrels-and-shearwaters/petrels/|access-date=2021-08-02|website=www.antarctica.gov.au|date=16 February 2017 |language=en|archive-date=2021-03-18|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210318101625/https://www.antarctica.gov.au/about-antarctica/animals/flying-birds/petrels-and-shearwaters/petrels/|url-status=live}} Breeding colonies during the October–November breeding period, can be as large as 200,000 pairs. However, the largest single colony ever observed, [[Mühlig-Hofmann Mountains]], was estimated to be about one million Antartic petrels, presumably including non-breeders and chicks.{{Cite journal |last=Mehlum |first=Fridtjof |last2=Gjessing |first2=Yngvar |last3=Haftorn |first3=Svein |last4=Bech |first4=Claus |date=1988-01-06 |title=Census of breeding Antarctic petrels Thalassoica antarctica and physical features of the breeding colony at Svarthamaren, Dronning Maud Land, with notes on breeding snow petrels Pagodroma nivea and south polar skuas Catharacta maccormicki |url=https://polarresearch.net/index.php/polar/article/view/2413 |journal=Polar Research |language=en |volume=6 |issue=1 |pages=1–9 |doi=10.3402/polar.v6i1.6841 |issn=1751-8369|doi-access=free }} They occasionally migrate to Australia or New Zealand in late winter. Unfortunately, this tends to occur when they're caught in a bad storm.{{Cite web|last=Miskelly|first=C. M.|date=2013|title=Antarctic petrel|url=http://nzbirdsonline.org.nz/species/antarctic-petrel|url-status=live|access-date=2021-08-02|website=New Zealand Birds Online|archive-date=2021-03-03|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210303182618/http://www.nzbirdsonline.org.nz/species/antarctic-petrel}} ==Behaviour== ===Food and feeding=== The petrel's diet is mainly krill, squid and small fish. Food is usually seized when the bird is on the surface but they also plunge-dive to obtain food, diving up to a depth of {{cvt|1.5|m}}. === Breeding === The Antarctic petrel breeding period is during October–November.{{Cite journal|last1=Lorentsen|first1=Svein-Hȧkon|last2=Amundsen|first2=Trond|last3=Anthonisen|first3=Kristin|last4=Lifjeld|first4=Jan T.|date=2000|title=Molecular Evidence for Extrapair Paternity and Female-Female Pairs in Antarctic Petrels|journal=The Auk|volume=117|issue=4|pages=1042–1047|doi=10.2307/4089648|jstor=4089648 |issn=0004-8038}} Each pair lays a single egg, which they incubate for 45–48 days after which there is a 42-47 day nestling period. Both members of the pair incubate the egg, 4% of pairs are female-female. Eggs have a 70-90% hatching rate. The two main causes of egg loss were predation by [[South polar skua]]s, and an egg rolling out of the nest and freezing. Antarctic petrel chicks rely on their parents for food as well as warmth.{{Cite journal |last1=Bech |first1=Claus |last2=Mehlum |first2=Fridtjof |last3=Haftorn |first3=Svein |date=1991-08-01 |title=Thermoregulatory abilities in chicks of the Antarctic Petrel (Thalassoica antarctica) |journal=Polar Biology |language=en |volume=11 |issue=4 |pages=233–238 |doi=10.1007/BF00238456 |bibcode=1991PoBio..11..233B |s2cid=30123748 |issn=1432-2056}} The [[physiological condition]] of the parent petrel dictates the amount of food it provides to its chick.{{Cite journal |last1=Tveraa |first1=Torkild |last2=Sether |first2=Bernt-Erik |last3=Aanes |first3=Ronny |last4=Erikstad |first4=Kjell Einar |date=September 1998 |title=Regulation of food provisioning in the Antarctic petrel; the importance of parental body condition and chick body mass |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1046/j.1365-2656.1998.00234.x |journal=Journal of Animal Ecology |language=en |volume=67 |issue=5 |pages=699–704 |doi=10.1046/j.1365-2656.1998.00234.x |bibcode=1998JAnEc..67..699T |issn=0021-8790|url-access=subscription }} Provisioning by parent petrels depends on both their own body condition and their chick's needs. Parent petrels in better body condition were more likely to have a chick that survived, and were able to increase the amount of food they gave to a smaller chick in a [[cross-fostering]] experiment. Chicks become thermally independent after day 11 post-hatching. ==Status== This petrel has an estimated occurrence range of {{convert|77500000|km2|sqmi|0|abbr=on}} and between 10 and 20 million adult birds. Due to its huge range and large numbers, it has been classified by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] as a species of [[least concern]]. ==References== {{Reflist }} ==External links== * [http://www.kiwifoto.com/galleries/birds/antarctic_petrel/ Antarctic petrel photos] {{Austrodyptornithes|P.|state=collapsed}} {{Taxonbar|from=Q771949}} [[Category:Procellariidae|Antarctic petrel]] [[Category:Birds of Antarctica]] [[Category:Birds described in 1789|Antarctic petrel]] [[Category:Taxa named by Johann Friedrich Gmelin|Antarctic petrel]]

zh.extract:
南极鹱（学名：Thalassoica antarctica）是一种羽色深棕和白色间杂的鹱，分布在南极洲。其种加词“antarctica”意为“南极洲的”。以磷虾、鱼和小型乌贼为食。他们通常边游泳边觅食，也可以从水面或空中潜下海面。


外观
成鸟的头部、体侧和背部都为棕色。鸟喙为黑色，腿为黄色。身体腹部和尾巴则呈白色，二级飞羽大部分为白色，上有棕色的斑点。


分布和生境
如其名，南极鹱生活在南极洲的岛屿和附近的海洋上。


參考資料
